---
bibliography: "quantificational-logic.bib"
number-sections: true
crossref:
  chapters: true
---

{{< include /assets/macros/macros.qmd >}}

# Quantificational Logic

## Introduction

Thus far in this course, we have studied two distinct systems of logic:
syllogistic logic and propositional logic. The first allowed us to recognize as
valid such arguments as:

Every Chicago winter is cold. Some Chicago winters are not snowy. Therefore, not
all seasons that are cold are snowy.

Propositional logic, on the other hand, taught us how to recognize the validity
of arguments such as:

The cat is either on the bed or under the bed. The cat is not on the bed.
Therefore, the cat must be under the bed.

But what about the following argument?

Every student in the class is either right- or left-handed. Some students in the
class are not left-handed. Therefore, some students in the class must be
right-handed.

The validity of this argument turns not only on the significance of such
syllogistic constructions as 'Every $\ldots$ is ---' and 'Some $\ldots$ is ---',
but also on the meaning of propositional operators, like 'either $\ldots$ or
---' and 'not $\ldots$'. Thus, to formulate standards for argumentative validity that
can apply to such arguments, we need a single system of logic that can comprehend
_both_ syllogistic and propositional modes of reasoning.

In this chapter, we introduce such a system of logic. We will call this system
'quantificational' logic. Another standard name for this system is first-order
predicate logic. In addition to mixed syllogistic-propositional reasoning, the
language of quantificational logic will also allow us to do more than merely
ascribe properties to objects. It will also allow us to express relations that
obtain between objects, so that we can recognize as valid such arguments as:

This capacity for relational reasoning, makes the logic
very powerful.

$$\forall x (L(x)\vee R(x)), \exists x \neg L(x)\proves\exists x R(x)$$

{{< include introduction.qmd >}}

## References {.unnumbered}

$$
$$
